Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 296 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 4.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: The RIGHT actions stopped early after 3 actions (requested 5). The player hit something. Let me check the current state to see where the player is and what's blocking.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After RIGHT 3:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] != 'w':
        b = n['boundary']
        center = (sum(p[0] for p in b)/len(b), sum(p[1] for p in b)/len(b))
        print(f"  ({n['color']}): center={center}, pixels={n['pixels']}, children={n['children']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The player is still at (34, 13.5)! The RIGHT actions didn't move the player. The pink bar decreased from 35 to 34 pixels. So the RIGHT action is being treated as a step that decreases the pink bar but doesn't move the player because the player is blocked.
Wait, that's strange. The player was at (34, 13.5) and tried to go RIGHT 3 times but didn't move. The pink bar decreased from 35 to 34. So each RIGHT action that's blocked still decreases the pink bar by 1.
Actually wait, looking more carefully: the player is at (34, 13.5) and the c frame is at (29.5, 17.5). The player is at col 13.5 and the c frame is at col 17.5. So the c frame is to the right of the player, but at a different row (29.5 vs 34). The player is at row 34 and the c frame is at row 29.5, so they're not on the same row.
But the player didn't move RIGHT at all. Maybe the player is blocked by something else. Let me check if there's an obstacle to the right of the player.
Actually, looking at the segmentation, the W frame at (30, 13.5) has 16 pixels and contains the blue at (29.5, 13.5). This W frame is at row 30, col 13.5. The player is at row 34, col 13.5. So the W frame is above the player, not to the right.
Let me think about this differently. The player is a 3x4 object (12 pixels) centered at (34, 13.5). So the player occupies rows 32-35 and cols 11.5-15.5 (approximately). The c frame at (29.5, 17.5) is a 3x4 object (12 pixels) centered at (29.5, 17.5), so it occupies rows 27.5-31.5 and cols 15.5-19.5.
The player's right edge is at col 15.5 and the c frame's left edge is at col 15.5. They might be touching! So the player can't move RIGHT because the c frame is blocking.
Wait, but the player is at row 34 and the c frame is at row 29.5. The player occupies rows 32-35 and the c frame occupies rows 27.5-31.5. They don't overlap in rows, so they shouldn't be blocking each other.
Hmm, let me reconsider. Maybe the player's shape is different. The player has 12 pixels and is centered at (34, 13.5). If it's a 3x4 rectangle, it would occupy rows 32.5-35.5 and cols 11.5-15.5. But the center is (34, 13.5), so the rows would be 32-35 (3 rows) and cols 11.5-15.5 (4 cols). That's 3*4=12 pixels.
Actually, I think the player is a 3x4 rectangle. The c frame at (29.5, 17.5) is also a 3x4 rectangle. The player is at (34, 13.5) and the c frame is at (29.5, 17.5). The player's right edge is at col 15.5 and the c frame's left edge is at col 15.5. But the player's top edge is at row 32 and the c frame's bottom edge is at row 31.5. So they don't overlap.
Wait, maybe the player's shape is different. Let me check the boundary of the player node.
Actually, I think the issue is that the player is blocked by the c frame at (29.5, 17.5) because they're diagonally adjacent. Or maybe the player can only move in certain directions.
Let me try moving UP instead to see if the player can move.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player at (34, 13.5). Try UP to see if player can move
# Check player boundary
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        print(f"Player boundary: {n['boundary']}")
        print(f"Player center: {sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']):.1f}, {sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']):.1f}")

# Try UP
action(['UP'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
